summary: Gruber reviews OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas browser after a week of use and finds it uncompelling — essentially Chrome with a chat button bolted on, despite OpenAI's claims of reimagining the browser. He argues that replacing search results with chat responses makes browsing slower and worse, that browser agent mode raises serious security and privacy concerns, and that the real purpose of Atlas appears to be harvesting users' personal browsing context for OpenAI's benefit. He highlights Anil Dash's critique that Atlas inverts the agent relationship — you become the agent for ChatGPT, not the other way around. Gruber concludes by noting that OpenAI's browser ambitions are fundamentally at odds with Apple's app-centric approach to personalized AI, and that Apple's advantage lies in mobile platforms where people live through native apps, not browsers.
key_points: Atlas replaces search results with chat responses, making web queries slower and less useful than traditional search engines like Google or Kagi
The browser's real purpose appears to be giving ChatGPT access to users' personal browsing context and digital lives rather than providing a better browsing experience
OpenAI's browser ambitions directly conflict with Apple's strategy of using native apps and App Intents as the personal knowledge layer for AI features
Text-based chat commands are a regression for web browsing — the visual point-and-click web won out over text-based interfaces for good reason
Apple's structural advantage is that most people live their digital lives on phones through native apps, not in desktop browsers
money_quotes: After giving it a try over the last week, to me Atlas feels like … Chrome with a chat button bolted on.
ChatGPT is many things but a good search engine replacement it is not. But that seems to be the entire premise of Atlas.
The point of it doesn't seem to be to provide a better web browser for me to use, but rather, to provide ChatGPT with the personal context of my digital life that it otherwise couldn't get.
OpenAI's ambitions are clearly at odds with Apple's.
We had an entirely text-based Internet before the World Wide Web, and the point-and-click visual metaphor of the Web won out.
I've never before seen a web browser offer any sort of incentive like this for making it your default. This promotion strikes me as simultaneously clever and icky.
Apple doesn't even have an LLM chatbot of its own, let alone a good or popular one.
key_insight: ChatGPT Atlas is less a browser built for users and more a Trojan horse designed to give OpenAI access to the personal browsing context it otherwise couldn't reach, putting OpenAI's ambitions directly at odds with Apple's native-app-centric AI strategy.
